FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 1, 2009

Bill Condrashoff
Amador Water Agency Director
11180 Ranchette Drive
Jackson, CA 95642

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-09-072

Dear Mr. Condrashoff:

This letter responds to your request for follow up advice regarding your duties as
a director with the Amador Water Agency Board ("AWA") under the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that our advice is based
solely on provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any,
of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict-of-interest or Government
Code Section 1090. In addition, the Commission will not advise with respect to past
conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A), copy enclosed.) Therefore, nothing in this letter
should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any
conclusions provided apply only to prospective actions. Also note our advice is based
solely on the facts presented in your request; the Commission does not act as a finder of
fact when it provides advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTIONS

1. May you participate in discussions and decisions involving classification of
water lines as transmission and distribution lines, including lines that will run across your
property?

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All
statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California
Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-09-072
Page No. 2

2. Are you required to leave the room while a matter for which you have a
conflict of interest is being considered in open session?

3. May you participate in a decision to endorse the designation of a local river as
"wild and scenic," a proposal initiated by the Foothill Conservancy, which is a source of
income to your business?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Because your property is directly involved in the decision, you may only
participate if there will be no financial effect on you or your property, not even one
penny.

2. The only statutory provision in the Act that requires an official to leave the
room during open session when a matter for which he or she has a conflict of interest is
being considered is Section 87105. However, Section 87105 only applies to persons
holding positions that are listed in Section 87200 and would not apply to water board
members. However, local rules may require a member to leave when the member has a
conflict of interest, and we cannot advise on local rules.

3. Because Foothill Conservancy is applying for the designation, you may only
participate if there will be no financial effect on Foothill Conservancy, not even one
penny.

FACTS

According to your prior letters:

• You are a director with the AWA. You were elected in November 2008 and took
office on December 5, 2008.

• The agency has five elected directors and provides water and wastewater service
within Amador County.

• AWA owns and operates the Canal, a 23-mile water conveyance system. The
Canal is open in most sections and is either lined with gunite or just earthen.
There are also piped sections of the Canal along its course. The Canal transports
untreated water from Pacific Gas and Electric's Tabeaud Reservoir to the
Agency's Tanner Water Treatment Plant. After treatment, the water is distributed
to the communities of Jackson, Sutter Creek, Ione, Amador City, Drytown, and
their vicinities. The Canal passes through your property and is also centered
along one boundary line of your property.

• AWA is working on the Amador Water System Transmission Project
("Transmission Project") to replace the Amador Canal. The project consists of an

File No. A-09-072
Page No. 3

8-mile pipeline from Tabeaud Reservoir to the Tanner Treatment Plant. This
pipeline is complete and does not run through your property. The stated goals of
the Transmission Project include improving water delivery to the Tanner
Treatment facility, improving water quality, and addressing problems relating to
service to existing customers along the Canal.

• The project also involves dewatering the Canal and laying smaller pipeline in the
Canal to continue serving existing water users along the Canal. You own 88 acres
of land (one parcel) in Amador County. Your residence is located on this
property. However, the small diameter pipeline would be installed in the Canal
through your property along the path of the Canal. You do not use water from the
Canal.

In your March 18, 2009, request for follow up advice, you provided the following
new facts:

• The AWA will be considering the classification of the new pipeline that will run
through your property. This line will be either a transmission line or a
distribution line. The classification of the lines will determine which customers
will pay the cost of the line. You stated the cost of the line will be paid by most
of the system customers.

• The Foothill Conservancy (the "conservancy") is a nonprofit organization and has
paid your printing business (owned 50% by you and 50% by your spouse) $1,227
in the past 12 months. The conservancy has been lobbying the AWA to consider
endorsing the designation of a local river as "wild and scenic." The AWA cannot
designate a river "wild and scenic" because federal law limits this power to
Congress, or the Secretary of the Interior (if the river is a part of a state river
protection system and the state governor applies to the Secretary). (16 USCS §
1273.)

• A conservancy official stated that if the decision is approved they may lose
funding for the purpose of pursuing the designation. The conservancy has total
annual receipts between $100,000 and $1,000,000.

ANALYSIS

In our prior letters to you we discussed the eight-step standard analysis for
deciding whether an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation
18700(b).) In those letters we concluded that you are a "public official" under the Act
who would be making, participating in making, or otherwise using your official position
to influence governmental decisions in satisfaction of the first two steps of the analysis.
In this letter you request guidance with respect to three specific decisions:

File No. A-09-072
Page No. 4

1. Discussions and decisions involving classification of water lines as
transmission and distribution lines.

2. A decision to endorse the designation of a local river as "wild and scenic."

As a follow-up to this earlier analysis you ask whether the Act requires you to
"leave the room when a matter for which you have a conflict of interest is being
considered. Section 87105 of the Act provides that certain public officials must
identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision;
interest: (1) orally
(2) recuse himself or herself: and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion
and/or vote on the item. However, Section 87105 by its express terms only applies to
officials holding positions that are listed in Section 87200. Section 87200 provides the
following:

"This article is applicable to elected state officers, judges and
commissioners of courts of the judicial branch of government, members of
the Public Utilities Commission, members of the State Energy Resources
Conservation and Development Commission, members of the Fair
Political Practices Commission, members of the California Coastal
Commission, members of planning commissions, members of the board of
supervisors, district attorneys, county counsels, county treasurers, and
chief administrative officers of counties, mayors, city managers, city
attorneys, city treasurers, chief administrative officers and members of
legislative bodies of cities, and other public officials who manage public
investments, and to candidates for any of these offices at any election."

Because your position is not listed in Section 87200, you are not subject to
Section 87105 of the Act.

However, Regulation 18702.1(a)(5) does provide that "[w]hen the determination
not to act [in a decision] occurs because of the official's financial interest, the official's
determination may be accompanied by an oral or written disclosure of the financial
interest." Thus, disclosure of the conflict of interest is permissive, but leaving the room
is not required. Finally, in a comment to Regulation 18702.1 the Commission stated:
"Nothing in this section authorizes or prohibits an agency by local rule or custom from
requiring a disqualified member to step down from the dais and/or leave the chambers."
Thus, you should be mindful of any local rule that may apply to you.

Step 3. What are your economic interests?

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising
from certain enumerated economic interests. In your prior letter, we focused on your
economic interest in real property. In this request you also ask a question that implicates

File No. A-09-072
Page No. 5

your interest in your business and sources of income to your business.² Section 87103(c)
provides that an official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, totaling $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.)

You stated that you are a 50 percent owner of a printing business. Pursuant to
§2030, because you are a 50-percent owner of the business, 50 percent of the
income to the business is treated as income directly to you. You stated that the
conservancy has paid your printing business $1,227 in the past 12 months. Your 50
percent share of that income would be $613.50. Because the conservancy has paid you
$500 or more in the 12 months prior to the decision, they are considered a source of
income to you under the Act.³

Step 4. Are your economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the
decisions in question?

Real Property: Under Regulation 18704.2, the general rule is that an official's
real property is considered directly involved in a governmental decision if, among other
criteria, the following applies:

"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any
part of that real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or
the proposed boundaries) of the property that is the subject of the
governmental decision;"

Additionally, real property is directly involved in a decision if the decision
involves the construction of, or improvements to, streets, water, sewer, storm drainage, or
similar facilities, and the real property in which the official has an interest will receive
new or improved services. (Regulation 18704.2(a)(6).)

As we discussed in our prior letter, because the pipeline will run right across your
property, your real property interest is directly involved in these decisions.

Sources of Income: Regulation 18704.1(a) states that a business entity or source
of income is directly involved in a decision before the official's agency when that
business entity or source of income, either directly or by an agent:

"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;

² You also have an economic interest in your printing business under Section 87103(a), (c) and
(d). However, your business is not implicated in the decision, and you have not asked us to analyze effects
on your business.
³ In addition, you have a community property interest in one-half of your spouse's income, so in
effect you will be attributed 75% of income to the business. (Seto Advice Letter, No. A-01-217.)

File No. A-09-072
Page No. 6

"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's agency. A
person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance,
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person."

You indicated that the "conservancy" has been lobbying the AWA to consider
endorsing the designation of a local river as "wild and scenic." Thus, the conservancy is
the applicant with respect to that decision and is directly involved.

Steps 5 and 6. What is the applicable materiality standard and is a material
financial effect reasonably foreseeable?

Real Property: A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably
foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official's economic
interest is material. (Regulation 18700(a).) Different standards apply to determine
whether a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an economic interest will be
material, depending on the nature of the economic interest and whether that interest is
directly or indirectly involved in the agency's decision.

For real property directly involved in a governmental decision, any financial
effect of the decision, even "one penny," is presumed to be material. (Regulation
18705.2(a)(1).) This presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably
foreseeable that the governmental decision will have any financial effect on the real
property. (Ibid.)

Sources of Income: The financial effect on sources of income that are nonprofit
entities is material if there is any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on the source of
income resulting from the decision. (Regulation 18705.3(a).)

Once a public official identifies his or her relevant economic interests, the official
must evaluate whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on those economic interests. An effect is considered "reasonably
foreseeable" if the effect is "substantially likely." (Regulation 18706; In re Thorner
(1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.) Whether the financial consequences of a governmental
decision are reasonably foreseeable at the time the decision is made is highly situation-
specific. A financial effect need not be a certainty to be considered reasonably
foreseeable. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably
foreseeable.

Under your facts, while the ultimate decision to make the local river "wild and
scenic" will have a financial effect on the nonprofit, it does not appear reasonably
foreseeable that the AWA's endorsement, by itself, would have such an effect.
Ultimately, however, the determination of whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the
applicable materiality standard will be met for your economic interests is necessarily a

File No. A-09-072
Page No. 7

factual question that is one for the official to make based the facts and circumstances
existing at the time of the decision. (Hensley Advice Letter, No. A-07-113; Moock
Advice Letter, No. A-01-140; O'Harra Advice Letter, No. A-00-174.)

Steps 7 and 8: The Public Generally and Legally Required Participation
Exceptions

An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate under the "public generally" exception. This exception applies when the
financial effect of a decision on a public official's economic interests is substantially the
same as the effect on a significant segment of the public. The "legally required
participation" rule applies when the official's participation in a governmental decision is
legally required. (Section 87101; Regulation 18708.)

You have not suggested that either of these exceptions are applicable to your
situation, therefore we do not address them here.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division

JWW:jgl

Enclosure

We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict-of-interest or Government Code Section 1090. In addition, the Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A), copy enclosed.) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions provided apply only to prospective actions. Also note our advice is based solely on the facts presented in your request; the Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it provides advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) QUESTIONS 1. May you participate in discussions and decisions involving classification of water lines as transmission and distribution lines, including lines that will run across your property? ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-09-072 Page No. 2 2. Are you required to leave the room while a matter for which you have a conflict of interest is being considered in open session? 3. May you participate in a decision to endorse the designation of a local river as "wild and scenic," a proposal initiated by the Foothill Conservancy, which is a source of income to your business? CONCLUSIONS 1. Because your property is directly involved in the decision, you may only participate if there will be no financial effect on you or your property, not even one penny. 2. The only statutory provision in the Act that requires an official to leave the room during open session when a matter for which he or she has a conflict of interest is being considered is Section 87105. However, Section 87105 only applies to persons holding positions that are listed in Section 87200 and would not apply to water board members. However, local rules may require a member to leave when the member has a conflict of interest, and we cannot advise on local rules. 3. Because Foothill Conservancy is applying for the designation, you may only participate if there will be no financial effect on Foothill Conservancy, not even one penny.
